core.when: When implementing currency calculations in financial data processing
core.action: Use floating-point types (float, double) for currency fields — floating-point representation causes rounding errors in monetary calculations

consequence.description: Floating-point precision errors accumulate in currency calculations, causing discrepancies in P&L reporting, margin calculations, and trade settlement amounts

core.when: When implementing financial data processing with currency amounts
core.action: Use Python's Decimal type with sufficient precision (minimum 4 decimal places) for each currency calculations, including prices, costs, fees, and P&L computations

consequence.description: Without Decimal, floating-point errors cause P&L discrepancies in backtests that don't appear in live trading, creating reproducibility failures and potential audit violations

core.when: When implementing custom exchange calendar for Algoseek data
core.action: Inherit AlgoseekCalendar from XNYSExchangeCalendar and only override open/close time methods — do not modify holiday logic or early-close handling

consequence.description: Overriding or replacing holiday logic causes backtests to execute trades on exchange holidays when no liquidity exists, producing non-reproducible results

core.when: When implementing prediction-to-price matching for backtesting
core.action: Extend prediction dates by 1 day so predictions made at close of day t are matched with actual prices at open of day t+1 — match pred_date with t+1 actual price

consequence.description: Without +1 day offset, predictions are matched to same-day closing prices, causing look-ahead bias where strategy appears profitable due to price data leakage

core.when: When accessing SeekingAlpha for earnings call transcripts
core.action: Use Selenium with Chrome/Firefox driver for login-based transcript access — verify webdriver initialization succeeds and session persists through navigation

consequence.description: Using stateless Scrapy for login-protected content returns authentication errors or empty responses, causing missing earnings transcript data in backtests

core.when: When configuring Algoseek data ingestion with Zipline bundles
core.action: Verify that minutes_per_day=1600 matches the Algoseek data schema (960 regular + 640 extended hours) — adjust only if using different Algoseek data products with different trading hours

consequence.description: Hardcoded 1600 minutes_per_day causes array size mismatch if Algoseek schema changes, resulting in data truncation or padding errors in backtests

core.when: When implementing feature normalization for ML model training
core.action: Use sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler (mean=0, std=1) for feature normalization — do not substitute with MinMaxScaler without validating outlier handling

consequence.description: Substituting MinMaxScaler without analysis causes gradient descent instability with outliers, degrading model accuracy and producing suboptimal trading signals

core.when: When running backtests with trained models
core.action: Assume the framework automatically binds model versions and data snapshots to backtest runs — it does not implement version tracking

consequence.description: Without explicit version binding, backtests cannot be reproduced when model or data versions change, causing discrepancies between reported and actual performance

core.when: When implementing backtest reproducibility
core.action: Implement explicit version tracking: record run_id, experiment_id, model_version hash, and data_version hash at backtest initialization and persist to metadata log

consequence.description: Missing version binding causes inability to reproduce exact backtest conditions, violating audit requirements and preventing debugging of performance discrepancies

core.when: When implementing settlement logic for trade execution
core.action: Assume the framework handles settlement conventions — the framework does not implement settlement date tracking or T+N settlement rules; trades are immediately settled

consequence.description: Without settlement convention handling, backtest results assume immediate settlement which does not match actual market T+1 or T+2 settlement rules, causing capital calculation errors and potential margin calls in live trading

core.when: When implementing order execution and position tracking logic
core.action: Track settlement_date = trade_date + settlement_days (default T+1 for stocks) and restrict re-trading of unsettled positions until settlement is confirmed

consequence.description: Without tracking settlement dates, the backtest allows same-day round-trip trades that violate settlement rules, causing capital imbalances that don't exist in live trading and producing misleading performance metrics

core.when: When implementing order price validation and fill simulation
core.action: Assume the framework handles tick size and lot size rounding — the framework does not implement tick/lot size precision; orders are filled at exact requested prices regardless of minimum increment

consequence.description: Without tick/lot size handling, order prices are not rounded to valid price increments, causing fill simulations to use prices that cannot execute in live markets and producing inaccurate slippage estimates

core.when: When implementing order price validation and fill simulation
core.action: Round order prices to nearest valid tick_size (e.g., price_tick = 0.01 for stocks, price_tick = 0.005 for options) and adjust order quantities to valid lot_size (e.g., lot = 100 for A-shares) before simulating fills

consequence.description: Without tick/lot size rounding, simulated fills use non-executable prices that differ from actual execution prices, causing systematic slippage estimation errors that accumulate over many trades

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for financial time-series models
core.action: Use TimeSeriesCV (purged walking forward split) instead of random K-fold or standard sklearn CV — verify no future information leaks into training via overlapping price data at train/test boundaries

consequence.description: Using random K-fold for time-series data causes look-ahead bias where future data influences training, producing CV estimates that are optimistically biased by 10-30% compared to actual live trading performance

validation_threshold: cv_strategy == 'KFold' OR cv_strategy == 'RandomSplit' → FAIL; cv_strategy == 'TimeSeriesCV' OR cv_strategy == 'PurgedWalkForward' → PASS

core.when: When preprocessing return features in the machine learning pipeline
core.action: Apply normalization/standardization to raw return values — returns are already in comparable scale and normalizing would destroy the magnitude information essential for distinguishing 5% moves from 0.5% moves

consequence.description: Normalizing returns makes economically significant moves indistinguishable from noise after scaling, causing the model to treat small fluctuations as equivalent to large price changes and degrading prediction accuracy for actionable signals

validation_threshold: feature_pipeline contains 'StandardScaler' OR 'MinMaxScaler' AND input includes 'returns' → FAIL

core.when: When implementing deflated Sharpe ratio calculation
core.action: Use Euler-Mascheroni constant exactly as emc = 0.5772156649 in the deflated Sharpe ratio computation — this constant appears in the asymptotic distribution of Sharpe ratio under non-normal returns

consequence.description: Using a different constant value corrupts the deflated Sharpe calculation, causing upward-biased performance attribution that leads to selecting strategies that underperform in live trading

core.when: When using the framework's default trading_cost_bps and time_cost_bps parameters for backtesting
core.action: Verify that trading_cost_bps=0.001 (0.1%) and time_cost_bps=0.0001 (0.01%) match the actual broker fee structure including spreads, commissions, and transfer fees; adjust to actual values if needed

consequence.description: Default trading costs of 0.1% may not reflect actual institutional or retail broker fees; overestimating costs penalizes legitimate trading, underestimating causes strategies to appear profitable when they are not

core.when: When implementing the Bailey-Lopez deflated Sharpe formula
core.action: Use np.e (approximately 2.718281828) as the correlation adjustment coefficient in the denominator: analytical_max_sr = expected_max_sharpe / (np.e ** (1 - 1/num_trials))

consequence.description: Using a different coefficient in the denominator invalidates the Bailey-Lopez correction for multiple testing bias, causing the deflated Sharpe to either over or under-correct, leading to strategy selection errors

validation_threshold: exp_coefficient != 1.0 OR formula_type == 'Bailey-Lopez' AND exp_coefficient != np.e → FAIL

core.when: When implementing the reinforcement learning trading environment preprocessing
core.action: Include the 'returns' column in sklearn.preprocessing.scale or any other feature scaling — returns must maintain original scale for correct reward signal interpretation

consequence.description: Scaling returns destroys the direct economic interpretation of the reward signal; the agent learns from distorted rewards and produces policies that make economically irrational trading decisions

core.when: When processing time-series data in the framework
core.action: Assume processing_time and as_of_time are equivalent or interchangeable — these represent distinct temporal concepts that must be tracked separately

consequence.description: Confusing as-of time with processing time causes look-ahead bias or stale data usage, corrupting backtest results and leading to strategies that cannot replicate live trading performance

core.when: When executing time-sensitive trading operations
core.action: Implement explicit distinction between as_of_time (event timestamp) and processing_time (system timestamp) with validation logic that flags mismatches and ensures correct temporal ordering

consequence.description: Without temporal validation, late-arriving data or out-of-order events cause incorrect state representation, leading to wrong trading decisions based on corrupted historical states

core.when: When implementing live trading data fetching logic
core.action: Assume fetched data is always fresh without stale data detection — the framework does not implement max_age or cache_ttl validation for live trading data

consequence.description: Without stale data detection, live trading may execute on outdated prices or fundamental data, causing orders to be placed at stale levels that have already moved, resulting in significant execution slippage

core.when: When implementing live trading data fetching and caching logic
core.action: Implement stale data detection by checking current_time > data_timestamp + max_age, and if cache_ttl is configured, verify freshness before use; mark stale data as non-tradable or trigger fresh fetch

consequence.description: Stale data in live trading causes orders to be placed based on outdated information, leading to adverse execution at prices that have moved away from the stale quote, accumulating losses over many trades

core.when: When implementing or refactoring dividend adjustment logic in backtesting
core.action: Preserve the explicit bypass of dividend adjustments as documented; do not automatically add dividend data processing without first verifying the broken implementation is fixed and tested

consequence.description: Skipping dividend adjustments means total return calculations exclude dividend income, making backtest results incomplete and potentially misleading for dividend-paying stocks and long-horizon strategies

core.when: When implementing web scraping middleware for data collection
core.action: Use configurable user agent rotation list from settings; do not hardcode a single user agent or remove the rotation logic during refactoring

consequence.description: Single static user agent increases blocking risk from target servers, causing data collection failures and gaps in alternative data pipelines that require consistent historical coverage

core.when: When implementing price data loading for large security universes in backtesting
core.action: Use ticker generator pattern for lazy loading that defers data fetching until each symbol is requested; do not replace with eager loading that loads the entire dataset into memory simultaneously

consequence.description: Eager loading of large security universes causes memory exhaustion when processing thousands of securities, preventing backtesting from completing and requiring expensive hardware upgrades

core.when: When implementing reinforcement learning episode initialization in trading environment
core.action: Use random starting position selection in reset() to introduce episode diversity across market regimes; do not refactor to use fixed starting points that create temporal correlation in training data

consequence.description: Fixed episode start points cause the agent to overfit to specific market regimes at the beginning of historical data, reducing generalization to unseen periods and creating temporal correlation in experience replay

core.when: When implementing train-test split logic in backtesting framework
core.action: Maintain lookahead gap between training window end and test window start equal to lookahead days (gap_size = lookahead); do not use zero-gap cross-validation that allows temporal overlap between train and test ranges

consequence.description: Zero-gap train-test split creates look-ahead bias where features at training end incorporate information unavailable at that time, making backtest results unreproducible in live trading

core.when: When implementing or modifying reinforcement learning environment reset and step logic
core.action: Maintain the asymmetry where reset() uses random starting index while take_step() uses fixed step increment; document this asymmetry explicitly to prevent unintentional changes that would destroy trajectory reproducibility

consequence.description: Randomizing step increments in take_step() would make trajectory analysis impossible for reproducibility verification, preventing debugging of learned policies and strategy auditing

core.when: When implementing reinforcement learning episode termination logic in trading environment
core.action: Use symmetric termination thresholds at NAV <= 0.0 OR NAV >= 2.0 (100% loss or 100% gain from initial NAV=1.0); do not replace with fixed episode length or asymmetric thresholds

consequence.description: Fixed episode length allows unlimited drawdown beyond 100% loss, removing bounded risk guarantees; asymmetric termination thresholds would distort reward shaping and risk management assumptions

core.when: When implementing feature pipeline with TA-Lib indicators for reinforcement learning
core.action: Preserve raw OHLCV data before computing indicators; implement logging of NaN/inf occurrence patterns to detect silent data loss from the cascade: TA-Lib produces NaN/inf for edge cases → dropna removes affected rows → raw OHLCV data is already gone

consequence.description: Silent data loss occurs when NaN/inf rows cluster around specific market regimes (e.g., low volatility periods), systematically eliminating training examples from those periods and causing model bias that accumulates silently

core.when: When calibrating trading cost parameters in reinforcement learning reward function
core.action: Perform sensitivity analysis across multiple bps values (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 20 bps) since the reward function reward = position * return - costs compounds costs multiplicatively with position size and every trade

consequence.description: Strategies appearing profitable at 10bps may be unprofitable at 15bps due to cost amplification through position-return products; incorrect cost calibration leads to strategies that look good in backtest but lose money in live trading

validation_threshold: cost_bps not in [5, 10, 15, 20] OR sensitivity_analysis == false → WARN

core.when: When selecting cross-validation methodology for financial time series models
core.action: Use TimeSeriesSplit or TimeSeriesCV for each financial ML model validation to preserve temporal ordering; do not use KFold with shuffle=True which destroys temporal structure and creates data leakage

consequence.description: Shuffled KFold creates optimistic, biased performance estimates by mixing future and past observations, while TimeSeriesSplit produces conservative, unbiased estimates; inconsistent CV methodologies lead to wrong strategy selection and deployment of overfitted models

validation_threshold: cv_method == 'KFold' AND shuffle == True AND is_financial_timeseries == true → FAIL

core.when: When implementing momentum signals in the DRL trading environment
core.action: Calculate Stochastic oscillator differential as slowD minus slowK (not absolute values) — slowD (%K smoothed twice) and slowK (%K smoothed once) crossover provides bullish/bearish momentum signals

consequence.description: Using absolute Stochastic values instead of differential eliminates crossover signal interpretation, causing the agent to lose critical timing information for trade entries and momentum divergence detection

core.when: When implementing momentum indicators in the DRL trading environment
core.action: Use Ultimate Oscillator with three lookback periods (7, 14, 28) — the multi-timeframe combination reduces false signals from single-period indicators by weighting short, medium, and long-term momentum

consequence.description: Replacing Ultimate Oscillator with single-period RSI loses multi-timeframe robustness, causing the agent to misinterpret short-term noise as momentum signals and execute trades at suboptimal entry/exit points

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for financial datasets
core.action: Use KFold with 5 splits and shuffle=True for balanced class distribution across folds; 5 splits provide 80/20 train-test splits balancing bias-variance tradeoff

consequence.description: Without shuffling, KFold may create imbalanced class distributions across folds, causing unreliable performance estimates that lead to deploying underperforming strategies

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for small financial datasets with severe sample constraints
core.action: Use LeaveOneOut cross-validation to maximize training data utilization when dataset is severely limited (<100 observations), but apply with caution due to high variance in estimates

consequence.description: LeaveOneOut with insufficient data produces high-variance performance estimates; strategies may appear to perform well but fail consistently in out-of-sample testing due to overfitting to individual observations

core.when: When implementing price-based features in the DRL trading environment
core.action: Normalize features using ATR (Average True Range) instead of standard deviation to properly handle gapped moves and daily price ranges across high and low volatility regimes

consequence.description: Standard deviation-based normalization fails to account for gapped overnight moves and true daily ranges, causing features to misrepresent volatility during market stress events and leading to poorly calibrated position sizing

core.when: When implementing return-based features in the DRL trading environment
core.action: Use 5 distinct lookback windows (1-5, 10, 21, 42, 63 days) to capture multi-scale price dynamics from short-term to longer-term trends; do not reduce to fewer windows

consequence.description: Single or reduced lookback windows cannot distinguish between short-term trend-following and medium-term mean-reversion opportunities, causing the agent to misinterpret market regime changes and execute inappropriate strategies

core.when: When setting episode length for DRL training in financial markets
core.action: Use 252 trading days (1 trading year) as episode length to capture seasonal patterns, earnings cycles, and macro-economic effects while maintaining computational feasibility

consequence.description: Shorter episodes lose seasonal patterns and earnings cycle information, while longer episodes increase return variance without proportional learning benefits

core.when: When implementing transaction cost modeling in the DRL trading environment
core.action: Use 10bps per-trade cost and 1bps per-period holding cost to reflect realistic institutional execution including spread, commission, and market impact

consequence.description: Underestimating transaction costs causes the agent to learn over-trading strategies that appear profitable in backtesting but incur unsustainable costs in live trading, leading to significant capital erosion

core.when: When defining the action space for a DRL trading agent
core.action: Use discrete SHORT/HOLD/LONG action space — this 3-action design captures directional market exposure without continuous position sizing complexity

consequence.description: Simplifying to 2-action space (UP/DOWN) eliminates short-selling capability, preventing the agent from profiting in bear markets or hedging long positions

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for reinforcement learning trading model evaluation
core.action: Use ShuffleSplit cross-validation to obtain Monte Carlo estimates with known variance properties — do not replace with single KFold which lacks variance estimation

consequence.description: Using KFold instead of ShuffleSplit loses variance estimation capability; without Monte Carlo averaging over random partitions, model selection becomes unstable and may choose suboptimal trading strategies

core.when: When selecting models based on alpha signal evaluation using financial returns
core.action: Use Spearman rank correlation for model selection — do not use Pearson correlation which assumes normality and linearity and is less robust for returns

consequence.description: Pearson correlation on non-normal financial returns can produce misleading correlation estimates, causing model selection to favor strategies with spurious correlations that fail in live trading

core.when: When comparing performance across multiple trading strategies in backtesting
core.action: Use Deflated Sharpe Ratio to adjust observed Sharpe for selection bias from multiple trials — do not use raw Sharpe ratio which ignores selection bias

consequence.description: Standard Sharpe ratio ignores selection bias from testing multiple strategies, causing overfitting where selected strategies appear to outperform but actually underperform in live trading due to lucky backtest selection

core.when: When processing buy/sell orders and calculating cumulative returns in backtesting
core.action: Assume PnL conservation is automatically implemented by the framework — the framework does not validate that cumulative PnL equals the sum of individual transaction PnLs

consequence.description: Without explicit PnL validation, rounding errors, slippage assumptions, or fee calculation bugs accumulate silently, causing backtest equity curves to diverge from actual trading results by 1-5% over time

core.when: When managing alternative data for production trading systems
core.action: Implement explicit PnL conservation checks: validate total_realized_pnl equals sum(position_pnl) at each rebalance point, and log discrepancies exceeding 0.01% tolerance

consequence.description: Without PnL conservation validation, fee calculation errors, rounding issues, or position tracking bugs cause silent capital erosion that compounds over thousands of trades

core.when: When loading model checkpoints and corresponding training datasets for production inference
core.action: Assume model/data version binding is automatically tracked by the framework — the framework does not enforce that model weights are paired with the exact dataset version used during training

consequence.description: Without version binding, using model weights with a different dataset version causes silent prediction drift; a model trained on normalized data receiving unnormalized inputs produces systematically incorrect trading signals

core.when: When managing alternative data in production trading systems
core.action: Implement model/data version binding: store model checkpoint with metadata including training_dataset_version, data_preprocessing_hash, and timestamp; validate version match before loading

consequence.description: Model/data version mismatch causes silent prediction failures where a model trained on specific data preprocessing receives incompatible input formats, leading to incorrect position sizing and trade execution

core.when: When working with financial data fields containing monetary values or quantities
core.action: Assume currency/unit annotation is automatically attached by the framework — the framework does not embed denomination metadata (USD, CNY, JPY) or unit specifications (shares, contracts) in data fields

consequence.description: Without currency/unit annotation, mixing JPY and USD values in calculations causes 100x errors, and treating shares as contracts creates position sizing errors that lead to margin calls or failed trades

core.when: When managing alternative data in production trading systems
core.action: Add explicit currency and unit metadata to each financial data fields: annotate denomination (currency_code field: USD/CNY/JPY) and measurement unit (unit field: shares/contracts/percent); validate no implicit assumptions across data sources

consequence.description: Missing currency annotations cause cross-border strategy failures where USD positions mixed with JPY data produce incorrect leverage calculations, triggering margin violations and forced liquidations

core.when: When computing expected maximum Sharpe ratio across multiple strategy trials for selection bias correction
core.action: Use analytical expected max Sharpe computation via extreme value approximation for fast computation; verify parameters n_trials, n_observations, sharpe_mean, and sharpe_std are passed correctly to the analytical formula

consequence.description: Incorrect parameter passing to analytical Sharpe computation produces wrong selection bias estimates, causing over-optimistic strategy rankings that select overfitted strategies

validation_threshold: n_trials parameter missing OR sharpe_std == 0 → FAIL

core.when: When validating Sharpe ratio selection bias correction in strategy backtesting
core.action: Use Monte Carlo simulation to validate analytical approximation when trial count or observation count deviates from asymptotic conditions; compare MC distribution with analytical estimate and flag discrepancies exceeding 5%

consequence.description: Relying solely on analytical approximation under non-asymptotic conditions produces biased DSR estimates, leading to strategy selection errors where overfitted strategies pass the bias correction test

validation_threshold: n_observations < 252 OR n_trials > 100 AND mc_validation missing → WARN

core.when: When initializing any stochastic component in ML4T workflows (DNN weights, dropout, sampling, data shuffling)
core.action: Set random seed to 42 before any stochastic operations to verify deterministic results across runs — do not run experiments without fixed seed

consequence.description: Without fixed random seed, stochastic training produces non-reproducible model parameters, causing strategy comparisons to be confounded by initialization randomness rather than actual strategy differences

core.when: When converting bundle data for decision tree/random forest model training
core.action: Use default interval '1d' for bundle conversion to verify daily OHLCV bars are used as primary granularity — do not assume intraday patterns are captured when interval is set to daily

consequence.description: Using minute-level data with daily-interval trained models causes feature mismatch where intraday patterns cannot be represented by daily-bar features, leading to systematically incorrect predictions

core.when: When implementing technical indicator calculations in backtesting
core.action: Use TA-Lib library for RSI, Stochastic, and other technical indicators — do not replace with custom implementations that may produce different numerical results

consequence.description: Custom implementations of complex indicators like RSI and Stochastic may produce different numerical outputs than the industry-standard TA-Lib, causing backtest results to diverge from professional trading systems and live trading signals

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for financial time-series models
core.action: Use TimeSeriesSplit for cross-validation to preserve temporal ordering — do not use KFold, GroupKFold, or shuffled splits that allow future data to leak into training sets

consequence.description: Using KFold or random splits in time-series cross-validation allows future information to leak into training, causing overly optimistic backtest results that do not reflect real-world prediction scenarios where only historical data is available

core.when: When implementing position representation and action space in trading environment
core.action: Maintain discrete 3-value position representation: -1 (short), 0 (neutral), 1 (long) — do not expand to continuous position sizing or add additional discrete values without retraining the agent

consequence.description: Expanding position representation from discrete -1/0/1 to continuous values [-1,1] changes the action space requiring full agent retraining; backtests using mismatched position interpretations produce incorrect P&L calculations and strategy signals

core.when: When evaluating and selecting alpha signals in quantitative strategy development
core.action: Use Spearman rank correlation for alpha signal evaluation instead of Pearson correlation — Spearman measures monotonic relationship robustness to outliers common in financial returns

consequence.description: Using Pearson correlation for alpha selection is sensitive to outliers in financial returns, potentially selecting inferior signals that perform well linearly but fail under real market conditions with fat-tailed distributions

core.when: When implementing web scraping for alternative data collection
core.action: Implement randomized delay between 7-9 seconds (8 +/- 1) between page requests — shorter delays risk IP blocking, longer delays excessively slow data collection

consequence.description: Using fixed delays or delays shorter than 7 seconds increases risk of IP blocking by target servers, halting all data collection; delays longer than 15 seconds unnecessarily extend collection time without reducing blocking risk

core.when: When implementing feature scaling in the trading environment's feature engineering pipeline
core.action: Use sklearn.preprocessing.scale for feature scaling — do not substitute with MinMaxScaler or RobustScaler

consequence.description: MinMaxScaler is sensitive to outliers common in financial data, causing feature distributions to be compressed toward the median and distorting model learning; RobustScaler adds complexity without sufficient benefit for this use case

core.when: When applying sklearn.preprocessing.scale to features in the trading environment
core.action: Explicitly exclude returns columns from sklearn.preprocessing.scale operations — returns must preserve their natural scale to maintain percentage change interpretation

consequence.description: Including returns in scaling destroys their natural distribution and magnitude, causing the RL agent to interpret normalized returns as dimensionless values rather than actual percentage changes, leading to incorrect position sizing decisions

core.when: When implementing return calculation for feature engineering in the trading environment
core.action: Calculate returns at horizons of 1, 2, 5, 10, and 21 days using pct_change — these specific horizons are mandatory for capturing both short-term noise and medium-term trends

consequence.description: Using different return horizons changes the temporal resolution of features, causing the RL agent to operate on different timescales than intended; finer granularity (hourly) increases computational cost without proportional benefit, while coarser horizons (60+ days) miss short-term trading signals

core.when: When implementing return feature calculation without explicit horizon specification
core.action: Verify that return horizons match the intended set [1, 2, 5, 10, 21] days rather than using default lookback values which may differ in timeframe coverage and miss relevant trading signals

consequence.description: Default return horizons may differ from the intended [1, 2, 5, 10, 21] selection, causing the model to train on signals at different timescales than live trading expects, leading to strategy underperformance in production

core.when: When implementing the reward calculation in the trading environment's step function
core.action: Calculate reward as start_position multiplied by market_return, then subtract costs — the formula reward = start_position * market_return - costs must be preserved exactly

consequence.description: Simplifying to absolute return only removes the agent's ability to learn position-direction awareness, causing it to take positions regardless of market direction; using Sharpe ratio adds complexity that obscures the direct profit-cost relationship the agent needs to learn

core.when: When implementing the RL action-to-position mapping in the ordering contract
core.action: Apply the mapping formula end_position = action - 1 where action 0 yields SHORT (-1), action 1 yields NEUTRAL (0), and action 2 yields LONG (1) — the offset of 1 is mandatory

consequence.description: Using direct action-to-position mapping or wrong offset causes all position decisions to be inverted or misaligned, making the RL agent take opposite positions from intended and systematically losing money on every trade

core.when: When implementing step-based cost calculation in the trading environment
core.action: Apply trading costs using `costs[max(0, step-1)]` — costs must appear one step AFTER the trade that generated them, not in the same step

consequence.description: Same-step cost application creates immediate feedback loops causing overly conservative policies; backtest results become inconsistent with delayed reward contract assumptions

core.when: When implementing reward calculation in the trading environment's take_step method
core.action: Calculate reward as `returns[t] * position[t-1]` — the previous position earns returns on the current market move, not the current position

consequence.description: Using position[t] * returns[t] assumes perfect execution speed unrealistic in live trading; backtest results become non-reproducible due to look-ahead bias from immediate reward recognition

core.when: When selecting the best alpha model for backtesting
core.action: Use Spearman rank correlation (scipy.stats.spearmanr) for model selection — maximize rho value, not MSE/RMSE; rank correlation captures monotonic relationship regardless of linear scaling

consequence.description: MSE/RMSE selection requires perfectly calibrated probability outputs; using these metrics instead of Spearman causes suboptimal model selection, leading to strategies that underperform due to scale-sensitive metric optimization

core.when: When processing datetime fields from alternative data sources (transcripts, earnings calls)
core.action: Assume datetime fields are timezone-aware or consistently formatted — the framework lacks timezone normalization to UTC, and naive datetime operations may silently produce incorrect results

consequence.description: Timezone mismatches cause off-by-one-day errors when comparing earnings across international markets; backtest timestamps become inconsistent with actual event dates

core.when: When parsing datetime from Seeking Alpha transcript titles or URLs
core.action: Parse dates using MM-DD-YY format for US date representations and Q1-Q4 patterns for fiscal quarters — do not replace with more flexible parsers like dateutil without explicit justification

consequence.description: Incorrect date parsing causes fiscal period misalignment; earnings data organized by wrong dates becomes non-comparable across years, breaking chronological analysis

core.when: When implementing transcript title parsing for Seeking Alpha data collection
core.action: Extract dates using MM-DD-YY format and fiscal quarters using Q1-Q4 patterns — these patterns enable automatic fiscal period identification essential for chronological organization

consequence.description: Changing to more flexible date parsers (dateutil) introduces complex error handling risks; incorrect date parsing breaks earnings data chronology and cross-year quarter comparisons

core.when: When implementing or modifying reward or cost calculation logic in DRL agent training
core.action: Verify reward and cost timing conventions remain synchronized with one-step delay (reward[t] = position[t-1] * returns[t], costs[max(0, step-1)]) — any modification to either timing contract requires updating both to maintain consistent temporal credit assignment

consequence.description: If only one timing contract is modified while the other remains unchanged, reward attribution becomes incorrect and policy gradients develop systematic bias, causing the agent to converge on suboptimal trading policies that either over- or under-estimate position values

core.when: When configuring cross-validation splits for time series backtesting
core.action: Set shuffle=True or alter date_idx ordering in MultipleTimeSeriesCV — temporal order must be preserved to prevent look-ahead bias

consequence.description: Shuffling or reordering splits destroys autocorrelation patterns in financial data, introducing look-ahead bias that makes backtested performance significantly overestimate actual live trading returns

core.when: When selecting cross-validation strategy for financial ML models in the same codebase
core.action: Use TimeSeriesSplit or TimeSeriesCV consistently across each financial ML tasks — avoid mixing with KFold + shuffle for different model types as this creates inconsistent validation approaches

consequence.description: Inconsistent validation approaches cause some models to leak future information while others respect temporal order, making strategy comparisons misleading and potentially selecting models based on artificially inflated performance metrics

validation_threshold: model_type in ['non-DRL'] AND cv_strategy == 'KFold' AND shuffle == True → WARN

core.when: When implementing asset lifecycle management and defining the trading period boundaries
core.action: Set auto_close_date to end_date + 1 day to verify assets remain tradeable throughout their designated period including the final trading day

consequence.description: Premature asset closure caused by using exact end_date would exclude valid trading opportunities on the last available data date, reducing backtest performance and misrepresenting actual trading potential

core.when: When configuring reinforcement learning trading episode termination parameters
core.action: Verify that episode termination thresholds (NAV=0 for loss, NAV=2.0 for 100% return win) match the specific strategy risk tolerance and capital requirements before using default values

consequence.description: Using default NAV termination thresholds without validation may cause premature episode ending or excessive computation on strategies with different risk profiles, leading to suboptimal training outcomes

core.when: When mapping security tickers between data sources for backtesting
core.action: Verify that NASDAQ symbols from pandas_datareader correctly map to the target data provider's symbol conventions; validate ticker coverage for the complete universe before assuming alignment

consequence.description: Incorrect ticker mapping caused by mismatched symbol conventions between data sources would cause security identification failures, resulting in missing or incorrect price data in backtest results

core.when: When implementing cross-validation for financial time series backtesting strategies
core.action: Verify training indices are chronologically AFTER test indices (train-after-test ordering); do not apply standard ML cross-validation patterns that would cause look-ahead bias

consequence.description: Using standard ML train-before-test ordering in time series CV causes data leakage, systematically overestimating strategy performance in backtests and producing results that cannot be replicated in live trading

core.when: When implementing or refactoring action-to-position mapping logic in the backtesting framework
core.action: Verify any refactoring of action-position mapping updates each occurrences of the mapping logic (action-1 offset) across the codebase simultaneously

consequence.description: Modifying only one instance of the action-position mapping causes positions to be inverted, making strategies take opposite positions than intended; live trading will execute inverse trades to backtest assumptions

core.when: When using adjusted close prices (adj_close) from Quandl Wiki or Algoseek data sources for backtesting dividend-paying stocks
core.action: Verify that the dividend adjustment pipeline is functioning correctly; if dividend adjustments are skipped or incomplete, explicitly document this limitation and exclude dividend returns from strategy performance metrics

consequence.description: Using adj_close while dividend adjustment is broken creates partially-adjusted prices, causing total return calculations to be understated for dividend-paying stocks; strategies expecting full dividend reinvestment will show lower backtest returns than actual live performance

core.when: When preparing backtesting data with adjusted close prices from Quandl Wiki
core.action: Use 'adj_close' field for Quandl Wiki prices to account for splits and dividends; do not substitute with unadjusted 'close' prices

consequence.description: Using unadjusted close prices during corporate actions causes backtest performance to be distorted; stock splits make per-share metrics appear artificially inflated or deflated, producing non-reproducible backtest results

core.when: When merging prediction data with price data for backtesting
core.action: Use 'right' join strategy when merging predictions with prices to retain each price observations; do not switch to inner join or left join without explicit justification

consequence.description: Switching to inner join excludes valid price points when predictions have gaps, causing the backtest to miss trading opportunities and underestimate strategy returns; left join creates gaps in price series affecting indicator calculations

core.when: When implementing walk-forward cross-validation with 126/21 training/testing splits
core.action: Verify that the effective training window accounts for the lookback-1 gap; explicitly calculate effective training size as (split_window - lookback_gap) and document this in the CV configuration

consequence.description: With 126/21 splits across 7+ folds, cumulative training data loss compounds to 7+ days per fold transition, causing the model to train on progressively smaller effective windows than expected, leading to underfitting in later folds and unreliable strategy evaluation

core.when: When running long crawling jobs for data collection
core.action: Implement post-crawl data completeness validation by checking for missing restaurants/dates after each batch, and retry failed URLs rather than marking as success

consequence.description: The Scrapy-Splash-random-delay cascade causes partial render failures to be marked as success, creating sparse datasets where some restaurants or dates are silently missing; accumulated partial failures distort statistical analysis and strategy signals based on incomplete data

core.when: When processing historical price data for stock splits
core.action: Run the stock split adjustment preprocessing step before any returns calculations; check that split_table is populated and applied to price columns using adjusted_price = raw_price / split_ratio

consequence.description: Unadjusted prices show artificial gaps at split dates, causing decision trees to make incorrect splits based on price discontinuities rather than true market movements, leading to strategies that would behave differently in live trading

core.when: When tracking historical security data through corporate actions
core.action: Use sid (security identifier) as the primary key for each historical lookups and portfolio tracking; implement ticker-to-sid mapping for user-facing queries while maintaining internal sid continuity

consequence.description: Using ticker-only tracking breaks historical continuity when companies rename or merge (e.g., GOOGL 2015 split), causing backtests to show discontinuous price history and incorrect position sizing across corporate actions

core.when: When implementing position sizing logic in the trading environment
core.action: Calculate end_position as action - 1, where action 0 produces short position (-1), action 1 produces neutral (0), and action 2 produces long position (+1); do not modify this offset formula

consequence.description: Changing the action-to-position mapping (e.g., to action+1 or lookup tables) inverts trading signals, causing short positions to become long and vice versa, leading to strategies that lose money in the opposite direction of expectations

core.when: When implementing signal-to-execution timing alignment in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework handles bar-level signal-to-execution timing alignment — the framework does not implement bar execution timing controls; signals at bar N may be executed at bar N instead of N+1

consequence.description: Without bar execution timing controls, signals execute at incorrect bars causing look-ahead bias or stale signal execution, leading to backtest results that cannot be reproduced in live trading

core.when: When implementing signal generation and execution logic in backtesting
core.action: Implement bar-level execution timing alignment — verify signals generated at bar N are executed at bar N+1 (next bar), and validate timing alignment in the execution layer with explicit timestamp matching

consequence.description: Without proper bar-level timing alignment, intraday strategies execute signals at incorrect price points, causing backtest equity curves to diverge materially from live trading performance

core.when: When implementing order management logic in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework tracks order state transitions automatically — the framework does not implement order lifecycle state machine; orders may proceed from pending to filled without proper state validation

consequence.description: Without an order lifecycle state machine, invalid state transitions (e.g., cancelled→filled) are not caught, causing order tracking errors and incorrect position calculations in backtesting

core.when: When implementing order management logic in backtesting
core.action: Implement an explicit order lifecycle state machine with valid states (pending, submitted, partially_filled, filled, cancelled, rejected) and validate each transitions; log each state change with timestamp and reason code

consequence.description: Without an explicit order state machine, order tracking errors accumulate silently, causing position discrepancies between backtest records and actual trade history that are difficult to audit

core.when: When implementing position sizing and risk management in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework enforces position sizing limits and aggregate risk caps automatically — the framework does not implement position size or risk ceiling controls; positions may exceed defined limits

consequence.description: Without position sizing and risk ceiling enforcement, strategies may accumulate oversized positions exceeding risk tolerance, causing catastrophic losses that backtesting fails to capture

core.when: When implementing order submission logic in backtesting
core.action: Implement position size validation against max_position_size per instrument and portfolio_risk_limit in aggregate; reject or scale orders that would breach these thresholds before submission

consequence.description: Without position size validation, orders may exceed per-instrument or portfolio-level risk limits, causing realized losses far exceeding backtested expectations in live trading

core.when: When implementing backtesting strategy validation
core.action: Assume the backtester detects and prevents overfitting automatically without explicit out-of-sample validation — the framework does not implement overfitting protection mechanisms

consequence.description: Without overfitting protection, strategies that appear profitable in backtest may be entirely fitted to noise, producing live trading results that are dramatically worse than backtested performance

core.when: When validating strategy performance before deployment
core.action: Implement out-of-sample validation by splitting historical data into training set and holdout set; only deploy strategies with Sharpe ratio degradation < 20% on holdout data compared to training data

consequence.description: Without out-of-sample validation, overfitted strategies pass backtesting but fail in live trading, causing sustained losses as the strategy trades on noise patterns that have no predictive power

core.when: When implementing finite difference option pricing models
core.action: Assume finite difference grids remain numerically stable without explicit stability validation — the framework does not implement grid stability checks; unstable grids produce meaningless option prices

consequence.description: Without grid stability validation, unstable finite difference grids produce oscillating or diverging option prices, causing systematic mispricing that leads to incorrect Greeks and flawed hedging decisions

core.when: When implementing finite difference option pricing models
core.action: Implement CFL stability condition validation: verify dt <= dx²/(2σ²S²) for each grid points before solving; if any cell violates stability, reject the grid with error and require parameter adjustment (reduce dt or increase grid points)

consequence.description: Without CFL condition validation, unstable grids produce nonsensical option values causing systematic hedging errors, with Greeks that may be orders of magnitude off from correct values
